caveats: a: OEWS occupation shares do not reveal task hours or current automation penetration.

a: Self-employed practitioners are excluded from OEWS, while the screened population is limited to transferable firms.

rho: AMA evidence covers physicians and health systems rather than independent rehabilitation practices.
rho: Evidence is strongest for physical-therapy documentation and weaker for the other professions in the code.

e: A qualifying revenue base may still depend on one clinician-owner or a concentrated referral source.

q: No source directly measures payer or clinician sharing of AI-enabled productivity.
q: Audiology device margins and speech or occupational therapy reimbursement may respond differently from physical-therapy visits.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Offices of Physical, Occupational and Speech Therapists, and Audiologists

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients receive evaluated plans and repeated treatment visits, commonly following referral or medical need; practices earn per-visit or per-service reimbursement from commercial insurers, Medicare, workers' compensation, and patients, with some audiology device revenue and contract services.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospital departments, skilled nursing and home-health providers classified elsewhere, captive employer clinics, software-only exercise products, pure hearing-aid retail, shell entities, and solo practices without transferable operations.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market independent and group outpatient practices providing recurring or repeat physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, audiology, and closely related rehabilitation services to external patients and payors.

a.bridge: BLS measures the exact industry's employment and wages rather than task hours: diagnosing and treating practitioners, therapy assistants and aides, and office-administrative staff dominate the workforce. I map scheduling, eligibility, intake, coding, billing, note drafting, chart review, home-program drafting, routine progress measurement, and communication to high exposure; evaluation and treatment planning to partial exposure; and hands-on examination, manual treatment, guarded exercise, swallowing work, device fitting, patient motivation, and safety accountability to low exposure. The result may overstate exposure if generated records require extensive correction and understate it if computer vision, remote monitoring, and automated exercise progression mature quickly.
caveats: OEWS occupation shares do not reveal task hours or current automation penetration.
The code combines professions with different hands-on intensity, documentation, devices, and reimbursement.
Self-employed practitioners are excluded from OEWS, while the screened population is limited to transferable firms.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study across physical, occupational, speech, and audiology practices separating direct treatment, evaluation, documentation, billing, scheduling, authorization, and home-program work, with audited AI time effects, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The administrative layer and clinician documentation are addressable, but a large wage share sits in licensed therapists and assistants delivering embodied, interactive care that software cannot substitute safely.

d5.bridge: BLS projects national employment growth for physical therapists, occupational therapists, speech-language pathologists, and audiologists over the coming decade. I weight the direction by their exact-industry employment presence and convert it into a moderated five-year constant-quality service-demand ratio, reflecting aging, chronic disease, surgery recovery, developmental needs, and hearing loss while discounting therapist shortages, reimbursement cuts, authorization limits, home-program substitution, and productivity changes. Employment can understate demand when vacancies persist and overstate service quantity when staffing intensity or documentation rises.
caveats: Occupational employment projections are not direct measures of constant-quality service demand.
National occupation growth includes hospitals, schools, home health, and other settings outside this code.
The code's discipline mix and payer exposure can shift materially over the horizon.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-industry constant-price receipts and visits by discipline, claims-based utilization per patient, referral volumes, wait times, vacancy rates, reimbursement changes, and home-program completion data would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Demographics and clinical need support growth across the principal disciplines, but realized visits remain sensitive to payer economics, referral patterns, licensed workforce availability, and patients' adherence and affordability.

caveats: No source measures eligibility inside the frozen lower-middle-market firm count.
The supplied count is margin-bridged using a broad healthcare-facilities margin that may misclassify practices.
A qualifying revenue base may still depend on one clinician-owner or a concentrated referral source.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Census product-line receipts or a commercial clinic directory matched to discipline mix, ownership, clinician count, payer mix, referral concentration, device sales, repeat visits, and founder dependence for the supplied population would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The code consists chiefly of independent private and group rehabilitation practices serving external patients, and repeated treatment episodes fit the recurring-service lens. The discount covers nontransferable founder practices, mixed home-health or institutional operations, audiology businesses dominated by retail devices, captive clinics, misclassification, and practices whose referrals or credentials would not survive a sale.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621340 ($25,528,793,330) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621340 ($45,314,241,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $97,549 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621340); class <5: 19,498 firms, avg revenue/firm $219,208 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,635 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,169 firms, avg revenue/firm $664,181 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $104,941 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,624 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,193,863 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $188,630 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,575 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,111,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $491,574 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 460 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,045,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,061,158 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 361 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,374,413 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,433,157 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 821

caveats: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required quantity share.
Routine exercise progression and low-acuity speech or hearing support may migrate to self-service faster than expected.
Operator necessity does not ensure that today's independent-practice model retains the service rather than hospitals, home health, or integrated networks.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal claims and outcomes comparing clinic-led, hybrid, and software-only rehabilitation by diagnosis and discipline, including adherence, adverse events, functional gains, escalation, and payer coverage, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Remote monitoring, guided home exercise, speech applications, hearing-device software, and digital coaching can replace some low-acuity visits, but examination, diagnosis, manual treatment, guarded movement, swallowing and communication assessment, device fitting, progression, motivation, and safety remain accountable clinician functions. Profession-specific ambient-scribe guidance also treats AI as documentation support rather than a substitute for the provider.

q.bridge: U.S. Physical Therapy reports revenue and costs per patient visit, negotiated commercial reimbursement, and a Medicare rate reduction with a direct revenue effect. I map per-visit reimbursement to partial retention of implemented documentation and administrative benefits because rates do not automatically fall with internal cost, then discount for government fee cuts, commercial renegotiation, utilization review, authorization burdens, clinician compensation, audit risk, and the inability to multiply hands-on visits without licensed capacity. The large physical-therapy operator may have greater negotiating leverage and centralized overhead than lower-middle-market practices, while fixed schedules may shield small practices' cost savings in the near term.
caveats: The evidence is concentrated in physical therapy and one large public operator.
No source directly measures payer or clinician sharing of AI-enabled productivity.
Audiology device margins and speech or occupational therapy reimbursement may respond differently from physical-therapy visits.
q.change_evidence: Practice-level contracts and claims linked to net reimbursement, denials, authorization labor, clinician compensation, visit duration, utilization, and margins before and after AI deployment across all four disciplines would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Savings in documentation, revenue cycle, and scheduling can improve cost per visit, but payers control rates and authorization, and the direct-treatment bottleneck limits capacity capture. Durable benefit sharing should therefore be substantial but incomplete.

rho.bridge: APTA documents emerging physical-therapy ambient scribes that capture, transcribe, and summarize encounters into structured notes, while AMA's physician survey and health-system examples show strong relevance and deployment of documentation and administrative AI in adjacent clinical settings. I translate availability and adjacent-provider experience into five-year implementation across exposed rehabilitation workflows, then discount for smaller-practice integration capacity, protected health information, consent, EHR fragmentation, coding and medical-necessity audits, profession-specific templates, clinician review, and limited evidence for occupational therapy, speech therapy, and audiology. Large health-system adoption may overstate resources, while repetitive therapy notes and authorization work may accelerate practice-level uptake.
caveats: APTA guidance shows technology availability and governance needs, not representative adoption or labor removal.
AMA evidence covers physicians and health systems rather than independent rehabilitation practices.
Evidence is strongest for physical-therapy documentation and weaker for the other professions in the code.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of independent rehabilitation practices measuring approved AI use by profession and workflow, verified clinician minutes saved, review time, claim denials, documentation errors, privacy incidents, and adoption by practice size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Documentation and administrative tools fit existing workflows and are already the subject of profession-specific guidance, but broad implementation requires reliable clinical language, system integration, payer acceptance, and accountable review.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports cross-industry five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners. U.S. Physical Therapy reports completed acquisitions of controlling and shared interests in outpatient therapy practices and an ongoing multi-clinic acquisition strategy. I haircut owner intentions for noncompletion, internal transfers, nontransferable referral relationships, clinician departures, and transactions without control, while raising the midpoint relative to an unsupported all-industry translation because exact-sector strategic deal activity is directly observed. The public consolidator may overstate buyer demand and access, while retained seller equity may improve transfer success but complicate qualification as control.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions across industries rather than completed rehabilitation-practice transfers.
One public consolidator's strategy may not represent the full buyer universe or screened practice sizes.
Transactions with substantial seller rollover or management-service structures may not qualify as straightforward control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Closed U.S. outpatient rehabilitation transactions from a healthcare deal database matched to revenue, EBITDA, discipline, control percentage, seller rollover, referral concentration, and post-close clinician retention, plus broker listing outcomes, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Outpatient rehabilitation has demonstrated strategic buyers and partnership structures, and clinic teams, referrals, payer contracts, and local brands can transfer. Founder retention and shared equity are common, so not every deal is an immediate clean control transfer.

principal_driver: Repeated reimbursed visits create scalable clinic operations with addressable documentation and administration around a service that remains clinically necessary.
principal_weakness: Hands-on clinician capacity and payer-controlled reimbursement limit both the size and retention of AI-enabled productivity gains.
